verdict_prose: **Verdict — no security layer is operational across either component.**
framing: The table below maps each missing security boundary to the component it exposes and the representative findings that confirm the gap.

weaknesses.description: No authentication library is declared in `package.json`. Every HTTP endpoint on port 3000 is reachable by any anonymous caller — there is no credential check to bypass.

weaknesses.category: Input Validation and Untrusted Input Handling
weaknesses.description: `lodash@4.17.10` accepts prototype-polluting keys in `_.merge` calls (CVE-2019-10744), and no input validation library is declared. Every request body is a direct injection vector into shared prototype state.

`Dockerfile:4` runs `npm install` without `--ignore-scripts` and no `package-lock.json` is present, making the dependency graph non-deterministic and every build vulnerable to lifecycle-hook code execution. The base image uses a mutable floating tag with no digest pin.

findings.label: Supply chain code execution via npm hooks

weaknesses.description: No TLS is configured at any layer — the Dockerfile exposes port 3000 with `node server.js` and no reverse proxy or HTTPS setup is declared. All traffic including any credentials flows in plaintext.
The Dockerfile has no `USER` directive; the container process runs as UID 0. Combined with missing rate limiting, the container surface is both over-privileged and unbounded in resource consumption.

findings.label: No rate limiting on any endpoint
